  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Phishing URL Detection — Hypothesis & EDA

K-o-m-A

2026-04-16

library(tidyverse)
library(corrplot)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)

1. Problem Statement

1.1 Real-world scenario

A user clicks a link in an email, chat, or search result. The browser, a mail gateway, or a corporate proxy needs to decide before the page loads whether the destination is a phishing site. The cheapest signal available at that moment is the URL string itself — no DNS lookup, no HTML fetch, no rendering. If this string-only decision is good enough, the system can block or warn the user without paying any network cost; if it is not, the system has to fall back to slower page-level analysis.

The question this project tries to answer is therefore concrete: how much of the phishing signal is already in the URL, and what is the minimal feature footprint needed to detect it reliably? Demonstrating that a URL-only model is competitive justifies a fast first-line filter; showing where it breaks justifies the (more expensive) trust- and behavior-level checks that follow.

This question maps directly onto the two assignment scenarios we picked:

  • Scenario 2 — parametric vs. non-parametric models on the URL-only tier tells us whether linear models are sufficient or whether the URL string hides non-linear structure that only RF/SVM/GBM can exploit.
  • Scenario 3 — algorithmic vs. embedded feature selection on the full feature matrix tells us how few predictors we actually need, and which ones survive penalisation under known multicollinearity.

1.2 Dataset

The dataset (PhiUSIIL) contains ~235 k observations and 50 predictive features drawn from three signal families:

Family Nature Example features
Lexical URL string properties URLLength, NoOfSubDomain, ObfuscationRatio, IsHTTPS
Trust Domain / title consistency URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, DomainTitleMatchScore
Behavior Page-level content signals NoOfURLRedirect, NoOfiFrame, HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields

The Lexical family corresponds to the URL-only first-line scenario above; Trust and Behavior require progressively more network/page information and serve as the strong-baseline comparison.

2. Hypotheses

H1 (Scenario 2) — On the URL-string-only tier, non-parametric models extract significantly more signal than parametric models

Phishing URLs leave lexical traces in the URL string itself — unusual subdomain depth, digit-heavy paths, obfuscated characters — that do not require downloading the target page. We expect a model trained exclusively on Lexical features (URL string, no DNS lookup, no HTML parse) to achieve meaningful AUC, but with a larger parametric vs. non-parametric gap than any other feature tier. This gap arises because lexical patterns are non-linear (combinations of character counts, lengths, special chars) and linear models cannot fully exploit them. We will test this with at least three parametric methods (LR, LDA, Naive Bayes) and three non-parametric methods (RF, SVM-RBF, KNN/GBM).

H2 (Scenario 3) — Embedded feature selection retains substantially fewer features than algorithmic selection by collapsing collinear clusters

The full feature matrix contains severe multicollinearity (URL-length cluster with VIF > 1000, see Section 4.3) and several pairs with |r| > 0.7. We hypothesise that embedded methods (LASSO, ElasticNet, RandomForest permutation importance) will retain fewer features than algorithmic methods (forward/backward stepwise, RFE) on the same task, because embedded penalisation collapses each collinear cluster to one or few survivors while algorithmic search treats correlated features as independent candidates. We will report (a) how many features each method ultimately retains, (b) the overlap between the retained sets, and (c) which features lose statistical significance as collinear partners are added or removed.

3. Data Loading & Cleaning

raw <- read_csv(
  "PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Strip UTF-8 BOM that appears on the first column name
names(raw) <- map_chr(names(raw), ~ str_replace(.x, "^\ufeff", ""))

# glimpse(raw)
# Columns excluded from modelling (identifiers / free text)
ID_COLS <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")

# Binary target as factor
df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label_bin = factor(label, levels = c(0, 1),
                            labels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(ID_COLS))

# Feature matrix (numeric predictors only)
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("Observations:", nrow(df), "\n")
Observations: 235795 
cat("Predictors:  ", ncol(features), "\n")
Predictors:   50 
# ── Feature-family mapping (reused throughout EDA) ──────────────────────────
family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                    ~family,
  "URLLength",                 "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",              "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                "Lexical",
  "URLSimilarityIndex",        "Trust",
  "CharContinuationRate",      "Lexical",
  "TLDLegitimateProb",         "Trust",
  "URLCharProb",               "Trust",
  "TLDLength",                 "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",             "Lexical",
  "HasObfuscation",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",        "Lexical",
  "ObfuscationRatio",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",          "Lexical",
  "LetterRatioInURL",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "DegitRatioInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",        "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL","Lexical",
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL",     "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                   "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",         "Behavior",
  "HasTitle",                  "Trust",
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",     "Trust",
  "URLTitleMatchScore",        "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                "Trust",
  "Robots",                    "Trust",
  "IsResponsive",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",          "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",     "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",              "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",           "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",           "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",          "Behavior",
  "Bank",                      "Trust",
  "Pay",                       "Trust",
  "Crypto",                    "Trust",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",          "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                   "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",           "Behavior"
)

3.3 Feature Exclusion

Before EDA we remove three categories of features that would bias or trivialise downstream modeling. All decisions are based on feature semantics.

Category 1 — Computed probability / similarity scores

These features are not raw URL measurements. They are outputs of prior models or expert-designed scoring functions that already encode phishing-detection logic. Including them would mean classifying the outputs of another classifier, not the URL itself.

SCORES <- c(
  "URLSimilarityIndex",   # similarity to known-phishing URL patterns
  "TLDLegitimateProb",    # P(TLD is legitimate) from an external lookup
  "URLCharProb",          # P(character sequence) from a language model
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",# computed domain↔title consistency score
  "URLTitleMatchScore"    # computed URL↔title consistency score
)
cat("Computed scores to remove:", paste(SCORES, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Computed scores to remove: URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore

Category 2 — Redundant binary indicator

HasObfuscation is defined as (NoOfObfuscatedChar > 0). It carries no information beyond the count feature already in the dataset.

REDUNDANT_BINARY <- "HasObfuscation"
cat("Redundant binary to remove:", REDUNDANT_BINARY, "\n")
Redundant binary to remove: HasObfuscation

Category 3 — Linearly dependent ratio features

Each ratio below equals count / URLLength, where both the count and URLLength are already present. Keeping both introduces perfect linear dependence (VIF → ∞) without adding signal.

RATIOS <- c(
  "LetterRatioInURL",      # = NoOfLettersInURL   / URLLength
  "DegitRatioInURL",       # = NoOfDegitsInURL    / URLLength
  "ObfuscationRatio",      # = NoOfObfuscatedChar / URLLength
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL"  # = NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL / URLLength
)
cat("Linearly dependent ratios to remove:", paste(RATIOS, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Linearly dependent ratios to remove: LetterRatioInURL, DegitRatioInURL, ObfuscationRatio, SpacialCharRatioInURL 
EXCLUDE_COLS <- c(SCORES, REDUNDANT_BINARY, RATIOS)

df       <- df %>% select(-all_of(EXCLUDE_COLS))
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("\nFeatures after exclusion:", ncol(features),
    "(removed", length(EXCLUDE_COLS), "of", ncol(features) + length(EXCLUDE_COLS), ")\n")

Features after exclusion: 40 (removed 10 of 50 )
cat("Removed:", paste(EXCLUDE_COLS, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Removed: URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore, HasObfuscation, LetterRatioInURL, DegitRatioInURL, ObfuscationRatio, SpacialCharRatioInURL

Update the family map to reflect the reduced feature set:

family_map <- family_map %>%
  filter(!feature %in% EXCLUDE_COLS)

family_map %>% count(family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  family       n
  <chr>    <int>
1 Behavior    20
2 Lexical     13
3 Trust        7

3.4 Feature Tiers Used in Modelling

To test H1 we define three nested feature tiers; modelling code consumes them directly from this notebook so the tier definitions stay consistent.

tier_lexical  <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Lexical")  %>% pull(feature)
tier_trust    <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Trust")    %>% pull(feature)
tier_behavior <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature)
tier_full     <- c(tier_lexical, tier_trust, tier_behavior)

cat("URLOnly tier (Lexical, ",  length(tier_lexical),  " features)\n", sep = "")
URLOnly tier (Lexical, 13 features)
cat("Trust tier              (",  length(tier_trust),    " features)\n", sep = "")
Trust tier              (7 features)
cat("Behavior tier           (",  length(tier_behavior), " features)\n", sep = "")
Behavior tier           (20 features)
cat("Full tier               (",  length(tier_full),     " features)\n", sep = "")
Full tier               (40 features)

4. Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Data Overview

4.1.1 Class Balance

df %>%
  count(label_bin) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label_bin, n, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, 0.1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .1))) +
  labs(title = "Class distribution",
       x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Takeaway: The dataset is roughly balanced, so no resampling or class-weight adjustment is needed before modelling. Accuracy is a valid metric alongside AUC.

4.1.2 Missing Values

miss_summary <- features %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "feature", values_to = "miss_rate") %>%
  filter(miss_rate > 0)

if (nrow(miss_summary) == 0) {
  cat("✓ No missing values found in any predictor.\n")
} else {
  print(miss_summary)
}
✓ No missing values found in any predictor.

4.2 H1 — Lexical Features Show Class Overlap That Requires Non-Linear Models

H1 predicts that a URL-string-only detector (Lexical features) will show a larger parametric vs. non-parametric gap than other tiers, because Lexical patterns are non-linear combinations of character counts and URL structure that linear decision boundaries cannot fully capture.

4.2.1 Class-Conditional Distributions — Lexical URLOnly Tier

Two Lexical features with non-degenerate class-conditional distributions. Most other URL counts (NoOfObfuscatedChar, NoOfEqualsInURL, NoOfAmpersandInURL, NoOfDegitsInURL) collapse to a near-zero spike for both classes and carry no visible signal in a density view — they are summarised quantitatively in the SMD plot below instead.

cont_selected <- c("URLLength", "NoOfSubDomain")

df %>%
  select(label_bin, all_of(cont_selected)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label_bin, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(feature = factor(feature, levels = cont_selected)) %>%
  group_by(feature) %>%
  mutate(value = log1p(value)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(feature = paste0(feature, " (log1p)")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(value, fill = label_bin, colour = label_bin)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.4) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  labs(title = "Class-conditional density — URLLength and NoOfSubDomain",
       subtitle = "Heavy class overlap → linear boundary cannot separate; non-parametric models can carve local rules",
       x = NULL, fill = "Class", colour = "Class") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Takeaway (H1): Lexical features show substantial class overlap — phishing and legitimate URLs share similar URL lengths, digit counts, and special character patterns. This overlap is the evidence that linear models will underperform non-parametric ones on the URLOnly tier.

4.2.2 Discriminative Power by Family (SMD)

smd_df <- df %>%
  select(label, all_of(names(features %>% select(where(is.numeric))))) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(feature, label) %>%
  summarise(m = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), s = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label, values_from = c(m, s),
              names_glue = "{.value}_{label}") %>%
  mutate(
    pooled_sd = sqrt((s_0^2 + s_1^2) / 2),
    smd       = abs(m_1 - m_0) / (pooled_sd + 1e-9)
  ) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(smd))

ggplot(smd_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, smd), y = smd, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Standardised mean difference by feature and family",
       subtitle = "Higher = more discriminative between phishing and legitimate",
       x = NULL, y = "| SMD |", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

SMD distribution per family with Kruskal-Wallis test:

smd_family <- smd_df %>% filter(family != "Other")

ggplot(smd_family, aes(family, smd, fill = family)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "SMD distribution by feature family (after removing computed scores)",
       subtitle = "H1: Lexical SMD lowest → largest parametric gap expected at URLOnly tier",
       x = NULL, y = "| SMD |") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# Kruskal–Wallis test: do families differ in median SMD?
kw_test <- kruskal.test(smd ~ family, data = smd_family)
cat("\nKruskal–Wallis test (SMD across families):\n")

Kruskal–Wallis test (SMD across families):
cat("  χ² =", round(kw_test$statistic, 2),
    ", df =", kw_test$parameter,
    ", p =", formatC(kw_test$p.value, format = "e", digits = 2), "\n")
  χ² = 6.67 , df = 2 , p = 3.55e-02 
# Median SMD per family
smd_family %>%
  group_by(family) %>%
  summarise(
    n_features = n(),
    median_smd = round(median(smd), 3),
    mean_smd   = round(mean(smd), 3),
    max_smd    = round(max(smd), 3)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(median_smd))

Takeaway (H1): Lexical has the lowest median SMD (≈0.33) and the smallest total signal (≈4.5), while Trust (median ≈0.90) and Behavior (median ≈0.60, total ≈16) carry stronger per-feature signal. With weak per-feature evidence and visible non-linear structure (Section 4.2.1), the URLOnly tier is the setting where parametric models will struggle most — exactly where the parametric vs. non-parametric gap predicted by H1 should appear.

4.3 H2 (Scenario 3) — Multicollinearity Motivates Algorithmic vs. Embedded Feature Selection

H2 hypothesises that algorithmic and embedded feature selection methods will retain different feature sets on the Full tier. The EDA evidence is the collinearity structure: where do correlated clusters live, and how severe is the inflation? Embedded methods (LASSO, ElasticNet, RF importance) collapse each cluster to one survivor; algorithmic methods (forward/backward stepwise, RFE) treat correlated features as independent candidates and tend to retain more.

4.3.1 Intra-Family Correlation Matrices

We show correlation matrices for all three families to expose where the collinearity that drives H2 actually lives.

Lexical family

lexical_cor <- features %>%
  select(all_of(tier_lexical)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(
  lexical_cor,
  method  = "color",
  order   = "hclust",
  tl.cex  = 0.7,
  tl.col  = "black",
  col     = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
  title   = "Correlation — Lexical features",
  mar     = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0),
  addCoef.col = "black",
  number.cex  = 0.55
)

Trust family

trust_cor <- features %>%
  select(all_of(tier_trust)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(
  trust_cor,
  method  = "color",
  tl.cex  = 0.8,
  tl.col  = "black",
  col     = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
  title   = "Correlation — Trust features",
  mar     = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0),
  addCoef.col = "black",
  number.cex  = 0.7
)

Behavior family

behavior_cor <- features %>%
  select(all_of(tier_behavior)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(
  behavior_cor,
  method  = "color",
  order   = "hclust",
  tl.cex  = 0.7,
  tl.col  = "black",
  col     = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
  title   = "Correlation — Behavior features",
  mar     = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0),
  addCoef.col = "black",
  number.cex  = 0.55
)

Cross-family redundancy summary

# Helper: mean and max absolute correlation from upper triangle
cor_summary <- function(cor_mat) {
  vals <- cor_mat[upper.tri(cor_mat)] %>% abs()
  tibble(
    n_features = nrow(cor_mat),
    n_pairs    = length(vals),
    mean_abs_r = round(mean(vals), 3),
    median_abs_r = round(median(vals), 3),
    max_abs_r  = round(max(vals), 3),
    pct_above_05 = round(mean(vals > 0.5) * 100, 1)
  )
}

bind_rows(
  cor_summary(lexical_cor)  %>% mutate(family = "Lexical"),
  cor_summary(trust_cor)    %>% mutate(family = "Trust"),
  cor_summary(behavior_cor) %>% mutate(family = "Behavior")
) %>%
  select(family, everything()) %>%
  print()
# A tibble: 3 × 7
  family   n_features n_pairs mean_abs_r median_abs_r max_abs_r pct_above_05
  <chr>         <int>   <int>      <dbl>        <dbl>     <dbl>        <dbl>
1 Lexical          13      78      0.292        0.242     0.956         23.1
2 Trust             7      21      0.183        0.162     0.429          0  
3 Behavior         20     190      0.143        0.098     0.701          4.2

Takeaway (H2): Lexical is the most internally correlated family (mean_abs_r ≈ 0.29, pct_above_05 ≈ 23 %) — driven by the URLLength / NoOfLettersInURL / NoOfDegitsInURL count cluster. Trust and Behavior are far less internally correlated (mean_abs_r ≈ 0.18 and 0.14). This is the structural reason H2 expects embedded selection (LASSO, ElasticNet, RF importance) to drop more Lexical count features than algorithmic stepwise/RFE, which has no built-in penalty against keeping all correlated partners.

4.3.2 Concrete Collinear Pairs

num_features <- features %>% select(where(is.numeric))
cor_mat      <- cor(num_features, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

cor_long <- cor_mat %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("feat_a") %>%
  pivot_longer(-feat_a, names_to = "feat_b", values_to = "r") %>%
  filter(feat_a < feat_b, abs(r) > 0.7) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(r)))

cat("Feature pairs with |r| > 0.7 after cleanup:\n")
Feature pairs with |r| > 0.7 after cleanup:
print(cor_long, n = Inf)
# A tibble: 10 × 3
   feat_a                     feat_b                         r
   <chr>                      <chr>                      <dbl>
 1 NoOfLettersInURL           URLLength                  0.956
 2 NoOfDegitsInURL            URLLength                  0.836
 3 NoOfDegitsInURL            NoOfEqualsInURL            0.806
 4 NoOfAmpersandInURL         NoOfObfuscatedChar         0.786
 5 NoOfEqualsInURL            NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL 0.785
 6 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL URLLength                  0.783
 7 NoOfDegitsInURL            NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL 0.767
 8 NoOfEqualsInURL            NoOfObfuscatedChar         0.755
 9 NoOfDegitsInURL            NoOfObfuscatedChar         0.721
10 NoOfExternalRef            NoOfSelfRef                0.701

These are the concrete collinear pairs that algorithmic and embedded selection are expected to handle differently. Most live inside the Lexical count cluster.

4.3.3 Variance Inflation Factors

library(car)
Loading required package: carData

Attaching package: 'car'
The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    recode
The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    some
glm_vif <- glm(label ~ .,
               data   = df %>% select(label, all_of(continuous_features)),
               family = binomial)
Warning: glm.fit: algorithm did not converge
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
vif_values <- vif(glm_vif)

vif_df <- tibble(
  feature = names(vif_values),
  vif     = as.numeric(vif_values)
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(vif))

ggplot(vif_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, vif), y = vif, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5,  linetype = "dashed", colour = "orange") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 10, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Variance Inflation Factors — continuous features",
       subtitle = "URL-length cluster has VIF > 500 → motivates embedded vs. algorithmic feature selection (H2)",
       x = NULL, y = "VIF", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

cat("Features with VIF > 10 (severe):\n")
Features with VIF > 10 (severe):
vif_df %>% filter(vif > 10) %>% select(feature, vif, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  feature                       vif family 
  <chr>                       <dbl> <chr>  
1 URLLength                  1070.  Lexical
2 NoOfLettersInURL            535.  Lexical
3 NoOfDegitsInURL              90.6 Lexical
4 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL   10.2 Lexical
cat("\nFeatures with VIF 5–10 (moderate):\n")

Features with VIF 5–10 (moderate):
vif_df %>% filter(vif >= 5, vif <= 10) %>% select(feature, vif, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  feature           vif family 
  <chr>           <dbl> <chr>  
1 NoOfEqualsInURL  7.94 Lexical

Takeaway (H2): Removing the four ratio features in Section 3.3 was necessary but not sufficient. URLLength (VIF ≈ 1070), NoOfLettersInURL (≈ 535) and NoOfDegitsInURL (≈ 90) remain near-collinear because they measure overlapping URL-string properties. Stepwise/RFE will likely keep most of them; LASSO and RF importance are expected to retain only one or two. Quantifying that gap is the H2 modelling task. Binary features are excluded from the GLM-based VIF by construction.

4.4 Skewness Assessment (Scenario 2 preparation)

# Skewness for all continuous features
skewness_fn <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  n <- length(x)
  m <- mean(x)
  s <- sd(x)
  (n / ((n-1)*(n-2))) * sum(((x - m) / s)^3)
}

skew_df <- tibble(
  feature  = continuous_features,
  skewness = map_dbl(continuous_features, ~ skewness_fn(features[[.x]]))
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(abs_skew = abs(skewness)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_skew))

ggplot(skew_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, abs_skew), y = skewness, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Skewness of continuous features",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines at ±2 mark heavy skew; LR/LDA may struggle here",
       x = NULL, y = "Skewness", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Count features with |skewness| > 2
n_heavy_skew <- sum(skew_df$abs_skew > 2)
cat("\nFeatures with |skewness| > 2:", n_heavy_skew, "of",
    length(continuous_features), "\n")

Features with |skewness| > 2: 19 of 22 
cat("These are:\n")
These are:
skew_df %>% filter(abs_skew > 2) %>% select(feature, skewness, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 19 × 3
   feature                    skewness family  
   <chr>                         <dbl> <chr>   
 1 NoOfCSS                      464.   Behavior
 2 NoOfObfuscatedChar           205.   Lexical 
 3 NoOfJS                       140.   Behavior
 4 NoOfEqualsInURL              115.   Lexical 
 5 NoOfEmptyRef                 107.   Behavior
 6 NoOfAmpersandInURL           107.   Lexical 
 7 NoOfiFrame                    97.7  Behavior
 8 NoOfDegitsInURL               94.9  Lexical 
 9 NoOfPopup                     85.0  Behavior
10 NoOfExternalRef               65.9  Behavior
11 NoOfSelfRef                   60.4  Behavior
12 NoOfLettersInURL              58.4  Lexical 
13 URLLength                     53.3  Lexical 
14 LineOfCode                    53.1  Behavior
15 LargestLineLength             48.2  Behavior
16 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL    47.4  Lexical 
17 NoOfImage                     28.2  Behavior
18 NoOfQMarkInURL                 8.16 Lexical 
19 DomainLength                   2.51 Lexical

Takeaway (Scenario 2): Several count-based features are heavily right-skewed with extreme outliers. This has direct implications for model selection:

  • Parametric models (LR, LDA): will require feature scaling and log-transforms of skewed features to satisfy distributional assumptions.
  • Non-parametric models (trees, KNN, SVM): are robust to skewness but KNN and SVM still require standardisation for distance-based computations.

5. EDA Summary & Modelling Implications

5.1 Hypothesis support from EDA

Note on dataset separability: After removing computed scores and redundant features (Section 3.3), 40 raw features remain (13 Lexical + 7 Trust + 20 Behavior). Initial modelling shows that this reduced set still yields AUC ≈ 0.99 for all model types — the dataset is near-perfectly separable on the Full tier. H1 is therefore tested primarily on the URLOnly tier (13 Lexical features) where meaningful parametric vs. non-parametric differences are expected to emerge; H2 is tested on the Full tier, where the multicollinearity that drives the algorithmic-vs-embedded gap actually exists.

Hypothesis EDA evidence Testable in modelling?
H1 (Scenario 2) — non-parametric > parametric on URLOnly Lexical has lowest median SMD (≈0.33) and visible non-linear class structure (Section 4.2). Linear boundaries cannot exploit count interactions. Yes — compare AUC of LR / LDA / NB vs. RF / SVM-RBF / KNN-or-GBM on URLOnly tier
H2 (Scenario 3) — embedded retains fewer features than algorithmic Lexical mean_abs_r ≈ 0.29, four features with VIF > 10 (URLLength=1070, NoOfLettersInURL=535, NoOfDegitsInURL=91, NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL=10), 10 pairs with abs(r) > 0.7. Yes — compare retained-feature counts and overlap between LASSO / ElasticNet / RF-importance and forward-stepwise / backward-stepwise / RFE on Full tier

5.2 Implications for modelling scenarios

Finding Scenario 2 implication (H1) Scenario 3 implication (H2)
Near-perfect separability on Full tier Comparison focused on URLOnly tier where models actually differ Use Full tier; selection methods need a non-trivial pool of redundant features
Lexical SMD lowest + non-linear structure Largest parametric/non-parametric AUC gap expected on URLOnly Algorithmic methods may keep all weak Lexical features; embedded penalisation should drop them
Heavy skewness in 19 of 22 continuous features Log-transform + standardisation for LR/LDA; trees/RF unaffected Apply same transform before LASSO/ElasticNet (they are scale-sensitive)
URL-length cluster: VIF > 500 and abs(r) > 0.84 Use stable solvers / regularised LR for fairness Strongest test case for H2 — embedded should collapse cluster, stepwise should not
Trust binary features: low intra-correlation No special treatment All three Trust HTTPS-style flags likely retained by both selection families — informative-feature overlap
